summary: Zhuge Liang; submits the famous *Chu Shi Biao* (Memorial on Setting Out) to Liu Shan and launches the First Northern Expedition. The memorial - one of Chinese literature's most-quoted documents - exhorts Liu Shan to govern well and recommends the men around him by name. The Shu army crosses Hanzhong; Xiahou Mao is named as the Wei commander in front of them. The North-South war begins.
Xiahou Mao; the Wei commander sent against Zhuge Liang's first invasion. Inept; relies on his Cao kinship. Will be repeatedly outmanoeuvred through chapters 92-93.
Cao Pi dies. Aged 40. Hands the throne to his son Cao Rui with Cao Zhen, Chen Qun, Sima Yi, and Cao Xiu as regents. End of the chapter's Wei half.
Cao Rui; succeeds Cao Pi at the start of his reign. Era Taihe; era-changes ahead. The Wei emperor against whom Zhuge Liang's expeditions will be aimed.
Cao Rui (duplicate record).
Sima Yi; named regent at Cao Pi's death; despatched to govern Yongliang to control the western frontier - the post he will hold against Zhuge Liang's expeditions.
Cao Zhen; named regent at Cao Pi's death; senior Wei marshal.
Cao Zhen (duplicate record).
Cao Zhen (duplicate record).
Cao Xiu; named regent at Cao Pi's death.
Chen Qun; named regent at Cao Pi's death.
Chen Qun (duplicate record).
Hua Xin; senior in the Wei court at the transition.
Wang Lang; senior in the Wei court.
Wang Lang (duplicate record).
Zhong Yao; senior in the Wei court.
Cao Cao; named in passing in the recap.
Empress Zhen; Cao Rui's mother; named in passing in the succession scenes - her status (had been demoted by Cao Pi).
Lady Zhen; same individual - named in passing.

Zhao Yun; the chapter's veteran centerpiece. Insists on going north despite his age (over 70 now). Zhuge Liang appoints him vanguard commander. The 'old tiger' role he plays through the next several chapters.
Liao Hua; with Zhuge Liang's northern expedition.
Jiang Wan; Zhuge Liang's chief administrator.
Li Yan; named in the Memorial as a senior official; tasked with eastern defence.

Zhao Yun; the chapter's veteran hero. Now over seventy, still in command of the vanguard. Cuts down five Wei generals in succession in the opening engagement, including Han Qi's father Han De and Han De's four sons. The old tiger's last great battle; Liu Bei's voice from the past echoes ('Zilong is all guts').
Han Qi; the Wei vanguard officer whose father and brothers Zhao Yun kills.
Han Yao; one of Han De's sons; killed by Zhao Yun.

Zhuge Liang; manoeuvres around Xiahou Mao; takes Nan'an, Anding, and (in the chapter's centerpiece) is set up to take Tianshui - except for the young Wei prefect Jiang Wei, who sees through every plan and counters them. Zhuge Liang resolves to capture Jiang Wei rather than just defeat him.

Jiang Wei; first major appearance. Young, brilliant, filially devoted (refuses to abandon his mother), serves under Ma Zun the Tianshui prefect. Reads Zhuge Liang's plans like an open book and undoes them - the first man in many chapters to outwit Zhuge Liang on a single field.
Xiahou Mao; captured by Zhao Yun in the opening; Zhuge Liang uses him as leverage.
Ma Zun; Wei prefect of Tianshui; Jiang Wei's superior; will lose Tianshui through his own incompetence.
Liu Yang; minor figure.
Cheng Yu; named in passing - the old generation.
Cheng Yu (duplicate record).
Cheng Wu; minor figure named in passing.
Cui Liang; minor Wei figure; killed in the campaign.
Cao Rui; in the Wei court; receives despatches of the western disasters.
Cao Rui (duplicate record).
Deng Zhi; in Zhuge Liang's army.
Guan Xing; with the expedition.
Zhang Bao; with the expedition.
Zhang Bao (duplicate record).
Zhang Bao (duplicate record).
Zhang Bao (duplicate record).
Ma Chao; named in passing - dead.
Ma Dai; with the expedition.
Wei Yan; with the expedition.
Wei Yan (duplicate record).
Wu Yi; with the expedition.
Wang Ping; with the expedition.
Jiang Jiong; minor figure.
Liang Xu; Wei officer at Tianshui.
Yang Fu; Wei official.
Yin Shang; Wei officer.
Shentu Pan; minor figure named in passing.

Jiang Wei; the chapter's centerpiece. Zhuge Liang takes Tianshui by a complex feint that uses Xiahou Mao to denounce Jiang Wei to Ma Zun as a traitor; cornered, Jiang Wei surrenders to Zhuge Liang weeping. Zhuge Liang takes his hand and declares: 'Now I have an heir.' The transmission begins.

Zhuge Liang; recruits Jiang Wei. Then advances to confront Cao Zhen's army. The chapter's climax is the famous oration in which Zhuge Liang debates the elderly Wei minister Wang Lang from the saddle in front of both armies - calling Wang Lang a faithless old man who has betrayed the Han three times; Wang Lang dies of apoplectic fury on the spot, falling dead from his horse. The book's most iconic verbal kill.

Wang Lang; the chapter's victim. Old senior Wei minister; rides out to debate Zhuge Liang and lecture him into surrender; Zhuge Liang demolishes him in front of both armies; Wang Lang dies of rage from his horse. Three-time turncoat finally felled by words.
Wang Lang (duplicate record).
Cao Zhen; Wei commander on the front.
Cao Zhen (duplicate record).
Cao Zhen (duplicate record).
Cao Rui; in the Wei court.
Cao Rui (duplicate record).
Cao Zun; Wei officer.
Guo Huai; first major appearance - Cao Zhen's right hand; will be a constant presence in the Wei-Shu front to come.
Guo Huai (duplicate record).
Guo Huai (duplicate record).
Zhu Zan; Wei officer; killed in the engagement.
Ma Zun; loses Tianshui through his own paranoia about Jiang Wei.
Xiahou Mao; used by Zhuge Liang as the patsy for the Jiang Wei feint; eventually released back to Wei.
Dong Zhuo; named in passing in Zhuge Liang's oration as the original Han traitor that Wang Lang served.
Li Jue; named in passing in the same oration.
Guo Si; named in passing in the same oration.
Liu Biao; named in passing in the oration.
Yuan Shao; named in passing in the oration.

Liang Qian; minor figure.
Liang Xu; Wei figure.
Yin Shang; Wei officer.
Zhao Yun; with the expedition.
Shentu Pan; minor figure named in passing.

Sima Yi; the chapter's pivot. Recalled to active command at Cao Rui's order; immediately moves on Xincheng and crushes Meng Da's planned defection from the inside before it can take effect - riding day-night to surprise Shen Yi's mole-action; Meng Da is beheaded by his own subordinates Shen Dan and Shen Yi who switch sides at the last moment. Sima Yi's classic strike-first style; Zhuge Liang's intended ally inside Wei evaporates in a week.

Meng Da; the long-plotted defection back to Shu collapses. He had been corresponding with Zhuge Liang; Sima Yi reads the intercepted letters and arrives so fast that Meng Da has no time to act. Killed by his own officers Shen Dan and Shen Yi turning coat at the eleventh hour.
Meng Da (duplicate record).
Shen Dan; betrays Meng Da to Sima Yi; rewarded with Wei rank.
Shen Yi; betrays Meng Da to Sima Yi alongside Shen Dan.
Cao Rui; recalls Sima Yi to command; receives him in audience. The decision that changes the war.
Cao Rui (duplicate record).

Zhuge Liang; presses the campaign - takes the Qiang king's army into Shu's alliance when the Qiang king Cheliji invades Wei from the west on Zhuge Liang's request; defeats Cao Zhen's army at Chang'an. Sima Yi's emergence offstage will undo all of it.
Cao Zhen; defeated by Zhuge Liang.
Cao Zhen (duplicate record).
Cao Zhen (duplicate record).
Cao Zun; Wei officer.
Deng Xian; Wei officer; killed by Yue Xun in the Qiang battle. (Father of the future Wei general Deng Ai by genealogy - but the figure here is the older Deng family scion.)
Xu Huang; killed in this chapter - shot in the head by an arrow from Meng Da's men on Sima Yi's approach to Xincheng; dies of the wound. The last of Cao Cao's named generals.
Zhang He; under Sima Yi's command, the most senior of the Wei field generals.
Guo Huai; with Cao Zhen.
Guo Huai (duplicate record).
Guo Huai (duplicate record).
Jiang Wei; with Zhuge Liang's expedition; rising as protege.
Hua Xin; in the Wei court.
Zhong Yao; in the Wei court.
Sima Shi; Sima Yi's elder son; named here in the staff.
Sima Zhao; Sima Yi's younger son; named here in the staff. The two Sima brothers begin appearing as a tandem.
Sima Zhao (duplicate record).
Sun Zi; on the Wei council.
Cao Cao; named in passing in the recap.
Cao Pi; named in passing - already dead.
Li Yan; sends supplies forward.
Li Yan (duplicate record).
Li Gu; minor figure.
Liang Ji; minor figure.
Liang Ji (duplicate record).
Wu Qi; minor figure named in passing.

Zhu Zan; Wei officer.
Shentu Pan; minor figure.

Ma Su; the disaster at Jieting. Zhuge Liang sends him to hold the strategic pass of Jieting with explicit orders to camp on the road and dig in; Ma Su disobeys and pitches his camp on the high hill, certain his elevation will let him sweep down on attackers. Sima Yi's column under Zhang He surrounds the hill, cuts off the water, and lets the Shu garrison die of thirst. Wang Ping (kept apart with his own column on the road as ordered) tries to relieve and fails. The whole northern campaign collapses.

Wang Ping; the chapter's tragic-but-correct figure. Argues with Ma Su against the hilltop plan; obeys when overruled; positions his own column on the road as Zhuge Liang had originally ordered. When Ma Su's hill is besieged, fights to relieve; pulls back the survivors. Will testify against Ma Su at the court-martial.

Zhuge Liang; the empty fort. With Sima Yi advancing on Xicheng where Zhuge Liang has only a handful of clerks (all his fighting men committed elsewhere), Zhuge Liang opens the city gates wide, has the gates swept clean by old men, and sits on the wall playing the qin with two boys beside him. Sima Yi arrives, suspects a vast ambush behind the calm, and withdraws. The novel's most famous bluff. Then orders the army home and prepares to court-martial Ma Su.

Sima Yi; takes Jieting through Zhang He's siege of Ma Su; arrives at Xicheng; falls for Zhuge Liang's empty-city bluff and withdraws. The two great minds' draw - Zhuge Liang's wits saving him from Sima Yi's overcaution.
Zhang He; commands the Wei column that takes Jieting from Ma Su; isolates the hilltop, cuts the water, finishes the job.
Cao Zhen; on the Wei side.
Cao Zhen (duplicate record).
Cao Zhen (duplicate record).
Cao Rui; in the Wei court.
Cao Rui (duplicate record).
Xin Pi; on the Wei council.
Guo Huai; with the Wei command.

Guo Huai (duplicate record).
Deng Xian; Wei officer.
Li Gu; minor figure.
Jiang Wei; with the Shu expedition; commands a wing of the retreat covering Zhuge Liang's withdrawal.
Meng Da; named in passing in the recap of his death.

Shen Dan; on the Wei side.
Shen Yi; on the Wei side.
Sun Li; Wei officer.
Sima Zhao; with his father's army.

Yang Yi; Zhuge Liang's chief of staff.

Yang Yi (duplicate record).
Deng Zhi; with the expedition.
Gao Xiang; with the expedition; defeated near Liecheng.

Ma Liang; named in passing - Ma Su's brother; the chapter recalls Liu Bei's dying warning ('Ma Su's words exceed his deeds; do not give him important command') that Zhuge Liang ignored, to his bitter regret.
Ma Dai; with the expedition.
Zhao Yun; commands the rear guard on the retreat; his last great service - keeps the Shu army from being run down.
